schools.summary: Ellicott fixes Athaliah as Ahab's daughter and Jezebel's true heir; the 'seed royal' means all with a claim to the throne, mostly Ahaziah's own sons.
Henry reads the slaughter as David's lamp almost extinguished; Athaliah acts from ambition and from rage against God's promise, which none can disannul.
Clarke denounces the lust of reigning that destroys all natural affection; Wesley traces the horror to Jehoshaphat's ungodly marriage-alliance with Ahab's house.
Ellicott and Keil work the text: 'sons which were slain' means 'to be put to death'; the babe was hidden in the store-room of the beds and later within the Temple.
Gill: Jehosheba was Joram's daughter but not Athaliah's, for a high priest would not wed so idolatrous a mother's child.
Wesley: the promise to David was bound up in one life yet did not fail; a wicked king's marrying his daughter to a holy priest saved the royal line.
Ellicott: the boy was with Jehosheba in a Temple chamber, perhaps the high priest's residence, while Athaliah 'was reigning' — a settled usurpation.
Gill: hidden in a priests' chamber (not the holy of holies), the child was a year old when concealed and seven when crowned.
Clarke: the concealment succeeded because only priests had access to the Temple and the high priest managed all.
Ellicott and Keil read 'rulers over hundreds' as centurions of the royal (Carian/Courier) guard; Chronicles adds the summoning of Levites and heads of families.
Henry: the hidden heir appears in his seventh year like the hidden sons of God; Jehoiada acts by office, by kinship, and after seeking counsel of God, with all discretion.
Gill: the rulers of hundreds are priests and Levites of David's twenty-four courses, whom the high priest could gather without suspicion.
Clarke: Jehoiada had sounded them all beforehand, and now binds the officers by oath and shows them the king in whose cause they must rise.

debates.question: Are 'the rulers over hundreds' commanders of Levites or of the royal guard?

sides.position: officers of the priests and Levites of the temple courses

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read the three companies as the royal guard relieved on the Sabbath; two centres must be held at once, the palace and the Temple.
Gill: the men are Levites of David's twenty-four courses, the incoming and outgoing companies both at hand on the Sabbath.
Wesley identifies them as Levites entering to minister; Clarke sees the Sabbath chosen for concealment, the men armed quietly from the Temple's stores.

debates.question: Are the three companies Levites or the royal guard?

sides.position: the king's runners/guard on their Sabbath relief

schools.summary: Ellicott weighs Sur against Chronicles' Jesod and reads the closing hapax as 'repulse'; the gates are palace exits to be stopped against all comers.
Gill relays the tradition that the gate of Sur is the eastern 'gate of the foundation,' known by several names.
Wesley takes Sur as the chief Temple gate and the aim as barring Athaliah and her soldiers from breaking in.

debates.question: What and where is 'the gate of Sur'?

sides.position: a side exit of the palace court; 'Sur' or a corruption of Chronicles' 'Jesod'
the eastern/chief gate of the Temple

schools.summary: Gill: the outgoing Sabbath course was detained rather than sent home, and divided into two companies.
Henry explains the mechanism: the Sabbath overlap of David's courses gave a double force, the outgoing company freed to guard the king's person.
Wesley, citing 2 Chronicles 23:8, notes the detained company's special charge to guard the king while the rest hold the gates.
Keil: 'whoever presses into the ranks shall be slain'; the guard keeps unbroken formation and attends the king in all his movements.
Gill: they are to compass the king round about, every man armed from the Temple's stores.
Wesley reads 'the ranges' not as the ranks but as the walls fencing the temple courts.

debates.question: What are 'the ranges' within which the intruder is slain?

sides.position: the walls enclosing the temple courts

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil: the captains (centurions of the guard) mustered exactly as Jehoiada 'had commanded,' joining incoming and outgoing companies.
Henry: each man was given his place as well as his work, for good order accomplishes any great enterprise quickly.
Gill: the captains did all that Jehoiada commanded, bringing their men to the priest.
Ellicott weighs the singular 'spear' and 'arms' for 'shields'; Keil pictures the armed guard ranged about the king from corner to corner along the altar and house.
Gill: David's own spoils, dedicated and stored in the Temple, arm the men secretly and unsuspected.
Clarke confirms from Josephus that Jehoiada opened the Temple arsenal David had prepared and distributed its weapons.
Ellicott: 'the guard' is the Couriers, not Levites; they form two lines from south to north wall, at the altar and at the sanctuary, 'round about the king' being anticipative.
Gill: the guard stood armed round about the king exactly as ordered.
Keil and Ellicott set out crown, testimony, and anointing per Deuteronomy 17; Ellicott records the rabbinic dissent that 'the testimony' was a robe or phylactery.
Henry reads the four acts as crown (power), testimony (law), anointing (the Spirit, typical of Christ), and acclamation (consent); so Christ's kingdom is welcomed into the heart.
Clarke details the regalia and the first recorded clapping for joy; Wesley: the law is a 'testimony,' a witness of God's will put into the king's hand to rule by.
Gill: Jehoiada brought forth the king's son from his Temple chamber to be crowned.

debates.question: What is 'the testimony' given to the king?

sides.position: the book of the law / covenant-charter (Deuteronomy 17:18)
a royal robe or a phylactery on the coronet (rabbinic view)

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott restore the dropped 'and' (the people and the halberdiers) and note the palace stood close by the Temple, so Athaliah came quickly at the noise.
Henry: like Jezebel she rushed to her own ruin; 'whom God will destroy he befools,' and the greatest traitor was loudest to cry treason.
Gill: she heard the coronation shouts as the people flocked to see and praise the new king.
Ellicott reads the king 'on the stand,' a royal dais before the altar; the 'princes' are chiefs (or 'singers' in LXX), and 'the trumpets' are the priestly clarions.
Gill: the king stood 'by a pillar as the manner was,' the customary royal station of David's house in the Temple.
Clarke: the king stood on a pillar or tribunal where kings were proclaimed; 'treason' is from a root meaning to bind together, a conspiracy.

debates.question: Did the king stand beside a pillar or upon a raised platform?

sides.position: on a stand / tribunal reserved for the king before the altar
by a pillar, the customary royal station

schools.summary: Ellicott: the centurions ('officers of the host') convey the order; 'have her forth without the ranges' means escort her out between the ranks, slaying any who take her part.
Henry: the righteous judgment must not stain the holy place, so she is led out to die beyond the courts.
Clarke: she had pressed in among the guards, and her supporters were to be slain at once; Wesley calls the Levites the Lord's host, now the king's.
Gill takes the officers of the host to be commanders of the priests and Levites.
Keil and Ellicott read the Hebrew as 'they made room for her on both sides,' a lane parting for her exit; she died at the horse-entry adjoining the palace, not in the Temple.
Gill gives both senses — 'laid hands on her' or 'set a place for her' — preferring that they opened a way to take her outside the Temple.
Clarke traces the topography of the horse-gate by the king's stables where she was slain.

debates.question: Does the Hebrew say she was seized or given passage through parted ranks?

sides.position: 'they made room for her on both sides' — a lane parted for her exit
'they laid hands on her' (LXX and Vulgate)

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott distinguish the two covenants: a renewed Mosaic covenant binding king and people to the Lord, and a civil compact binding king and people to each other.
Henry: king and people covenant with God and with one another, the king level with his subjects; they first gave themselves to the Lord, then to each other (2 Corinthians 8:5).
Clarke and Wesley name a sacred covenant to be the Lord's and renounce idolatry, and a civil covenant of just rule and loyal obedience, the ground of prosperity in Church and state.
Gill: God renews his pledge to be their God as they root out idolatry and restore pure worship.
Keil records the thorough demolition and Mattan's death; Ellicott ties it to the covenant, sets Baal's house on the Temple mount, and explains the overseers appointed to guard true worship.
Henry: the covenant at once abolished idolatry; only Mattan clung to his altar, and 'there he was slain, the best sacrifice that ever was offered upon that altar.'
Clarke: Athaliah had set up Baal's worship as Jezebel did, her sons having plundered God's house for the Baals (2 Chronicles 24:7).
Gill locates Baal's temple, built by Athaliah, on the 'mount of corruption' near Jerusalem.
Keil and Ellicott map the triumphal procession from the Temple down to the palace by the gate of the Couriers, ending in the king's enthronement ('they seated him').
Henry: the king is brought in state to the palace and set on the throne of judgment, the thrones of the house of David.
Gill lists the escort of captains, guard, and people who conducted the king.

Keil gives the sequence of joy, quiet, and Athaliah's death; Ellicott takes 'the people of the land' as civilians accepting the change and reads 'Athaliah they had slain' as an emphatic backward glance.
Henry: the people feasted for joy, fulfilling Proverbs 11:10; the way to be joyful and at peace is to engage fully in God's service.
Gill: they rejoiced that David's nearly-quenched lamp — the line running to the Messiah — was set again on the throne, for God's promise cannot fail.
Clarke: the people were glad to be rid of Athaliah's tyranny, and the city was quiet, she having no partisans left to disturb the reign.
Ellicott notes the Hebrew editions join this verse to chapter 12, where the reign of Joash is taken up.
Gill reckons the arithmetic: a year old when his father died, six years hidden, and seven when he began to reign.
Clarke marvels that a child of seven should be made king, the first such instance on record.
